items.title: PageRank and the dominant eigenvector
items.scenario: Google's early PageRank models the web as a stochastic matrix P where each row gives transition probabilities between pages. The steady-state distribution π satisfies πP = π, so π is a left eigenvector with eigenvalue 1.
items.explanation: Power iteration repeatedly multiplies an initial distribution by P and normalizes; it converges to the dominant eigenvector, ranking pages by long-run visit probability.

items.title: Principal directions in customer data
items.scenario: A retailer has two correlated features per customer: total spend and visit frequency. The covariance matrix has eigenvectors pointing along directions of joint variation; the largest eigenvalue direction captures most spread for a 1D summary.
items.explanation: This is the core idea behind PCA: eigenvectors of covariance are principal axes; eigenvalues measure variance along each axis.

items.title: Recurrent network stability
items.scenario: A simple RNN updates hidden state h_{t+1} = W h_t. If the largest |eigenvalue| of W exceeds 1, small perturbations in h can explode over time; if all |λ| < 1, states contract to zero.
items.explanation: Spectral radius (max |λ|) governs long-term amplification or decay—relevant when reasoning about gradient explosion/vanishing in sequence models.
